title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CARB Prepares for 8th November Hearing Amidst Active Public Discourse around Proposed Amendments
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:Â 

(28th October â€“ 3rd November)Â Â 
  • A total of 85,282 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 14.1% rise week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades recorded a fall of 29.90% WoW as 583,995 credits were traded in CA LCFS.Â 
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $63.20/MTâ€¯for Type 1â€¯transfers, recording a 6.92%â€¯fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 9.50% in price.Â 
  • The average LCFS spot credit price rose by 0.28% WoW; on 31st Oct, it was $64.08/MT. The benchmark price stood at $64.56/MT on 31st Oct.Â 
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 1.86% WoW, while D4, D5, and D6 RIN prices each decreased by 1.43% WoW.Â 

cCarbonâ€™s Market ViewÂ 

The California LCFS market is strengthening, reflected in rising spot credit prices, coinciding with the upcoming November 8 CARB hearing. Expectations of a tighter market are driving this trend, as CARBâ€™s proposed amendments suggest stricter standards. However, on October 22, the Environmental Justice Advisory Committee (EJAC) and allied groups submitted a formal letter urging CARB to reject the amendments, citing concerns over credits for methane emissions from livestock, fossil-based hydrogen loopholes, biofuel volume limits, and electrification funding for heavy-duty vehicles. They claim that the proposal could allow long-term methane crediting, even under future livestock methane regulations.Â  Market volatility followed on October 23, with LCFS credit prices falling to $60.67, compounded by Republican lawmakersâ€™ calls to delay the hearing due to anticipated gas price increases of up to 85 cents per gallon by 2030, which could impact working-class Californians. Governor Gavin Newsom has since directed CARB to explore E15 ethanol blending, which could reduce gas prices by up to $0.20 per gallon, potentially saving Californians $2.7 billion annually without environmental compromises.Â  In sustainable aviation, BioVeritas announced its innovative BioVeritas Processâ„¢ for producing SAF, a breakthrough that allows conversion of biomass into SAF with fewer feedstock and energy limitations, promising substantial growth. Separately, CARB and Airlines for America (A4A) formed a partnership to boost SAF use in California to 200 million gallons by 2035, aiming to meet 40% of intrastate travel demandâ€”a tenfold increase from current levels.Â 

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume TrendÂ 

[caption id="attachment_589332" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 6.92% WoW fall from $67.90 to $63.20 this week. Additionally, all non-zero credit prices rose by 9.50% compared to the previous week. Type 1 trades saw 85,282 credits exchanged, recording a 14.1% rise WoW this week, but the total transaction volume for All Non-Zero Trades had decreased by 29.90% WoW this week, to reach 583,995 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_589333" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices increased by 0.28% WoW, reaching $63.60/MT. On 31st Oct, the average spot price was $64.08/MT, which rose from $60.67/MT, the lowest point in October. The benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2024, stood at $64.56/MT as of 31st Oct, up from $61.38/MT as of 23rdÂ Oct.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_589334" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 47 trades were reported in the market, with seven high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was at $78.00 for a transaction of Type 2 at a volume of 25,000 credits, and the lowest transfer by price last week was at $42.00 per credit of Type 2, reported at a volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 100,000 credits in Type 2 were undertaken for a price of $70.23, and the smallest volume transfers of 60 credits, a Type 1 category, were undertaken for a price of $66.00/MT.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_589335" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] , with volumes reaching 6.40 MMT, reflecting a notable 66.23% YoY increase compared to 3.85 MMT in October 2023. October recorded the highest monthly transaction volume of 2024, surpassing all previous months this year. Concurrently, Type 1 credit prices have sustained their upward trend initiated in July, averaging $65.93 in October, underscoring continued market resilience. By comparison, prices in October 2023 were approximately $70.12.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_589336" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 28th Â Â Oct â€“ 03rd Nov, total VPT recorded a 20.63% rise (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 1.86% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices decreased by 1.43% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Synthica Energy Breaks Ground on Renewable Natural Gas Facility in Rome, Georgia. Cincinnati-based Synthica Energy broke ground this month on a new Renewable Natural Gas (RNG) facility in Rome, Georgia, just outside of Atlanta. â€œSynthica Romeâ€ will be the first of its kind in the Greater Atlanta region and is expected to divert nearly 250,000 tons of waste from local landfills each year. Food and beverage manufacturers and other customers will have less distance to transport their pre-consumer waste, lowering costs and cutting down on emissions for them, while the plantâ€™s anaerobic digestion technologies will create RNG and remove thousands of tons of CO2 and methane from the atmosphere each year.
  • Growth Energy: 45Z Extension Bill Will Unlock Investment. Growth Energy, the nationâ€™s largest biofuel trade association, issued the following statement after Reps. Brad Schneider (D-Ill.), Dan Kildee (D-Mich.), and Julia Brownley (D-Calif.) introduced a 45Z extension bill. The Expanding Clean Fuel Production Act would extend the Section 45Z clean fuel production credit for 10 years. Passed as part of the Inflation Reduction Act (IRA), the 45Z clean fuel production tax credit is intended to incentivize the production of low-carbon fuels in transportation on the ground and in the air.
  • Convertus Celebrates Groundbreaking for the New Biofuel Facility in York Region. In a significant step towards a greener future, Convertus in collaboration with key partners, broke ground on the provinceâ€™s first large-scale biofuel facility to integrate liquid CO2 capture technology. Located in the Town of East Gwillimbury, in the Regional Municipality of York, this pioneering project will transform organic waste into renewable natural gas (RNG), helping to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and promote a sustainable energy future for one of Canadaâ€™s fastest- growing municipalities.
  • Vanguard Renewables Breaks Ground on its First Organics- to- Renewable Gas Facility in Virginia. Vanguard Renewables, a portfolio company of Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP), a part of BlackRock, today held a groundbreaking ceremony for its newest renewable natural gas project at The Moyer Familyâ€™s Oakmulgee Dairy Farm in Amelia Court House, VA. The farm, owned by Larkin Moyer, a fourth-generation dairyman, along with his sons Brandon and Jeremy and is the oldest continuously operating family dairy in the Commonwealth.
  • Governor Newsom Urges Accelerated Action on E15 Gas Blend to Lower Prices. Governor Gavin Newsom issued a directive to the California Air Resources Board (CARB) on 25th October, 2024 to expedite measures that could lead to lower gas prices without compromising environmental protections. Building on legislation passed in 2023 and 2024 to prevent price spikes and increase transparency in the oil industry, the Governorâ€™s order directs CARB to accelerate studying how California could increase ethanol blending in gasoline (E15), which could help to lower prices by up to $0.20 per gallon and save Californians as much as $2.7 billion every year â€” with little to no impact on the environment.
  • BioVeritas Launches into SAF, Soaring Past the Constraints Facing the Industry Today. BioVeritas, LLC, a leading sustainable fuels and biochemicals company redefining the future of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), announced that it is launching The BioVeritas Processâ„¢ for SAF. This breakthrough technology converts biomass via fermentation into volatile fatty acids (VFAs), a key precursor for a range of high-value products, most notably SAF.
  • CARB and Nationâ€™s Leading Airlines Announce Landmark Partnership For a Sustainable Aviation Future. California has reached an historic agreement with the nationâ€™s leading passenger and cargo airlines to dramatically accelerate the use of sustainable aviation fuel for flights within the state. In an announcement made today at San Francisco International Airport, the California Air Resources Board (CARB) and Airlines for America (A4A), an industry trade organization representing nearly a dozen major airlines, committed to a goal of increasing the availability of sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) for use within California to 200 million gallons by 2035, an amount that would meet about 40% of intrastate travel demand â€“ a more than tenfold increase from current levels.
